stories: Good morning Sean, it's Saturday, the eighteenth of April, 2026
On this exact day back in 1930, the BBC's evening news bulletin opened with the announcer saying, quite seriously, "there is no news"
The broadcaster simply played piano music for the rest of the fifteen-minute slot
Today we don't quite have that luxury, so let's get into it
Looking at your day, you're over in Saskatoon at the Sheraton Cavalier, marked as out-of-office and working Canadian hours
Your calendar's got a morning run pencilled in and otherwise a do-not-disturb block through the working day, so it looks like a focused one
Over in Singapore, a couple worth your attention
A postgraduate student was taken to hospital after a fire broke out in a research laboratory at Nanyang Technological University yesterday afternoon
Good morning Sean, it's Friday, the seventeenth of April, 2026
On this day in 1964, Ford unveiled the Mustang at the New York World's Fair â and 22,000 people put their names down to buy one that very first day
Your calendar is looking clear today â a clean run into the weekend
Over in Singapore, a couple of stories worth noting
It's a quiet infrastructure story but a meaningful one
The attack lasted several hours and drew immediate condemnation from European capitals
Crude has been above a hundred dollars a barrel for weeks, and a functioning Hormuz changes the supply picture considerably if the ceasefire holds
Moving to Germany, South Africa, and Australia
Good morning Sean, it's Thursday, the sixteenth of April, 2026
On this day in 1972, NASA launched Apollo 16 Ã¢ÂÂ John Young, Ken Mattingly, and Charles Duke headed for the Moon's Descartes Highlands
Young and Duke spent three days on the surface, collecting nearly 100 kilograms of lunar samples and driving the lunar rover further from the lander than any crew before them
Young later commanded the very first Space Shuttle mission
A fine reminder of what a well-planned Thursday can achieve
Over in Singapore, two stories this morning
The case is renewing international attention on Singapore's mandatory drug sentencing laws
In lighter news: Singapore's new two-power-bank-per-passenger aircraft rule came fully into effect yesterday, with 15 devices confiscated at checkpoints on the very first day
